methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $252.6B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
ARC Clean Technology is a Series G nuclear energy company that has raised $50M+. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of 11 peers: three public companies (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and eight private firms including Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy. However, the private peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or valuation-tier overlap to support a robust peer-median approach.

The stage-progression method is the applicable triangulation technique here. Using total capital raised as a proxy base ($252.6B), a typical Series G-to-next-stage step-up multiplier of 1.25x yields an implied valuation of $315.8B. This method assumes ARC's funding trajectory aligns with historical step-up patterns within the nuclear energy sector at this advancement stage.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $268.4B (low) to $363.1B (high), with a median point of $315.8B. This single-method triangulation carries material uncertainty: the lack of granular peer comparables in nuclear energy at Series G limits cross-validation, and the stage-progression multiplier depends heavily on the assumption that ARC's funding base accurately reflects its equity value. Any material divergence between capital-raised and equity value—driven by funding concentration, dilution structures, or sector-specific risk factors—would require adjustment to this range.
